context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: State

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of the State in development planning? Is the 'Third World State' expected to behave differently from the 'First World' State in its relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs? What is the impact of globalization on the State? What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda stuck on this reflection abt the State in development planning, and idk where to start. can u just explain the whole thing in a simple way first?
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to help you get started! But instead of me explaining it, let's build from what you already know.

In your own words — what do you think a State *is*, and what does it *do*? Don't overthink it, just give me your gut answer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole topic for them, which would violate the core rules of not giving answers directly and using the Socratic method. Instead, I should acknowledge their difficulty getting started and use a guiding question to activate their prior knowledge. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them do the thinking. Starting with what they already know about the State is the right entry point before diving into the nuances of the prompt.

exchanges.student: uhh ngl my gut answer is basically “the government,” but then I get lost bc idk how that’s diff from other actors. that broad explainer angle didn’t work lol, so can u tell me the main distinction super simply?
exchanges.tutor: "The government" is actually a solid starting point! Now think about this: what can a State do that, say, a private company or an NGO *cannot* do? What kind of power does only the State have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing again for a direct explanation, which I should decline. However, their answer ('the government') is actually a decent starting point and shows some basic understanding. I should validate that briefly and then use a Socratic question to push them toward the key distinction the prompt is asking about — what makes the State unique compared to other actors (legitimacy, rule-making power, coercive authority). I'll ask a targeted question to help them discover this themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe make laws n collect taxes? but then I’m still fuzzy on why that matters for development planning tbh. can u just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: You're actually closer than you think! Laws and taxes are great examples. Now — if the State is the one setting the rules, how does that affect what other actors (like businesses or NGOs) can do in a country's development? Why would that matter for a planner?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a good answer (laws and taxes) but is again asking for the big picture explanation. I should not provide it. Instead, I should validate their answer and use a Socratic question to connect their insight directly to development planning. The link between law-making/rule-setting power and planning is exactly what the prompt is asking about — the State as the 'rule-maker.' I'll help them make that connection themselves rather than explaining it.

exchanges.student: idk, like businesses follow rules maybe, NGOs too, but I still can’t see the full essay shape rn. that angle’s not clicking either—can u give me a basic outline I could use?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write an outline for you — that's your job! But notice what you've already figured out: the State makes laws, collects taxes, and sets rules that other actors must follow. That's actually a core idea for your essay.

So here's a simpler question: based on that, what would you say makes the State *different* from other actors in planning? Try putting it in one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide an outline, which would be completing the task for them — a clear violation of the rules. I should firmly but kindly decline and redirect. However, I notice the student is struggling to connect the dots, so I should help them see that they've already built useful building blocks in this conversation. I'll point that out and ask them to try articulating the connection themselves rather than giving them a structure.

exchanges.student: maybe: the State is diff bc it has legit authority to make and enforce rules for everyone else. but fr I’m still blanking on the First World vs Third World part.
exchanges.tutor:
That sentence is genuinely good — hold onto it for your essay.

Now, on the First World vs Third World question: think about a developing country you know of. Does its government have the same *capacity* and *freedom* to make decisions as, say, the US or Germany? What might get in the way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated a solid core idea about the State's distinguishing feature (legitimate authority to make and enforce rules). I should affirm this clearly. They're now moving naturally to the next part of the prompt — the First World vs Third World State distinction — which is good progression. I should use a Socratic question to help them think about this themselves rather than explaining it. A good entry point is asking them to think about what constraints or pressures a developing country's State might face that a wealthy State doesn't.

exchanges.student: maybe less money, weaker institutions, more pressure from donors or global markets idk. can u tell me if that means the State is expected to do more or less?
exchanges.tutor:
Those three points are excellent — especially external pressure from donors, that's a key tension in the course.

Here's the thing: some argue the Third World State *should* do more (invest, intervene, build), while others argue it should do *less* (step back, let markets work). Can you think of *why* someone might argue each side?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a strong answer with three good points (less money, weaker institutions, external pressure from donors/markets). I should affirm these clearly. They're asking me to resolve the 'more or less' question for them, which I shouldn't do directly. Instead, I should turn it back to them — the answer actually depends on which actor you're comparing to, and the tension between doing more (developmentalist state) and doing less (neoliberal pressure) is exactly the dilemma the prompt is pointing to. A good Socratic question will help them see this tension.

exchanges.student: hmm maybe “do more” bc markets won’t fix poverty/infrastructure, and “do less” bc states can be corrupt or inefficient. ngl I kinda just want a sample thesis now.
exchanges.tutor: I won't write a thesis for you, but honestly — you just *said* the thesis. You have the tension, you have both sides, you have the State's unique role. Try writing your own thesis sentence using what you just told me. Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed strong building blocks: the State's unique legitimacy, the constraints on Third World states, and the tension between interventionist and neoliberal views. They're asking for a sample thesis, which I must refuse as it would be doing the writing for them. However, I should note they have enough material to try writing their own thesis now. I'll push them to attempt it themselves using what they've already figured out.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: the State is central bc it has legitimate authority, but in developing countries it faces extra limits from weak institutions and globalization, so it’s expected to intervene while also being pushed to step back. is that decent?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis — it hits the key tension well. One thing worth developing: you mention globalization, but *how exactly* does it constrain or change what the State can do? That could strengthen your argument significantly. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good thesis that captures the core tension of the prompt. I should affirm this clearly without grading it. They've now covered the State's unique role, the First/Third World distinction, and the globalization angle. The prompt also asks specifically 'what is the impact of globalization on the State?' which they've touched on but could develop further. I should nudge them toward that piece and also remind them they now have enough to start writing. We're at a good transition point to move toward drafting.

exchanges.student: maybe trade rules, IMF/World Bank pressure, foreign investors, outsourcing stuff? I’m still not sure how to turn that into body paragraphs tho.
exchanges.tutor:
Good examples — IMF/World Bank conditionality is especially relevant to the course themes.

You now have strong ideas on every part of the prompt. Rather than planning more, I think it's time to *start writing*. Take what you've built here — the State's unique authority, the Third World constraints, the globalization pressures — and draft your opening paragraph. Show me what you've got.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified good examples of globalization's impact on the State. They're now asking about body paragraph structure, which is getting close to asking me to outline the essay for them. I should not do that. However, we've now covered enough ground in conversation — the student has solid ideas on all three parts of the prompt. This is a good moment to push them to start writing rather than continuing to plan verbally. We're also approaching the end of the planned conversation length, so I should encourage integration of what we've discussed into actual writing.

exchanges.student: ugh I’m still not ready tbh, can u just show me one possible intro paragraph so I can see the vibe?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write a paragraph for you — not even a sample. That's your work, not mine.

You have a thesis, you have examples, you know the core tension. The only thing left is to write. It doesn't have to be perfect — just start with your thesis sentence and go from there. Post your attempt and we can work from that.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a sample paragraph for them, which I must firmly refuse — this is exactly what the rules prohibit. The student has all the building blocks they need and has been avoiding writing for several exchanges. I should be direct and clear that I won't do this, remind them of my role, and firmly push them to write their own draft. Being less pleasing and more direct is appropriate here.
